desc:
Packed mud tracks with a wide strip of grass between them mark this portion of
the Greenway. To the east, the billowing mounds of the South Downs rise and fall
in long ridges like trees fallen on the green plains. West of the road, a small
patch of forest marks the most significant vegetation for miles.
name: Greenway

desc:
The dark earth of the Greenway makes another winding turn through the hills to
the east and the patch of forest to the west. Beyond the forest, more hills
rise, leaving little else on the horizon. To the east, a low valley lies between
the ridges.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The road makes a sharp turn here as it winds around the forest to the north and
west and the hills to the east. Two brown ruts have been dug deep by the biting
turn of heavy-laden wagons. A circle of rocks off one corner of the road
indicates this is a popular spot to rest and build a fire.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
To either side of the road, the large hills of the South Downs rise like the
walls of a canyon. The eastern hills are steep and rocky, making passage
impossible, while the western slopes have a much more forgiving grade. Some of
the road has begun to wash away, apparently caused by the rain running off the
adjacent hills.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road here is hard, packed dirt. Only a few small pebbles can be seen pushed
off to the sides of the road. Thin, knee-high weeds grow tenaciously where they
can avoid wagon wheels and boots. The ground slopes down slightly to the west
and into a field. Hills to the east block the way.
name: Greenway

desc:
The greenway curves here and can be followed west or north. A smaller path leads
off to the east without any sign indicating what waits at the end. Hills can be
seen far off in almost every direction. A narrow gorge can be seen to the south.
name: Greenway

desc:
As the road curves it begins to be sheltered by a cliff wall to the west. Hills
descending from the northwest allow the road to carve through this area
maintaining an easy grade. A field is to the north and a trail branching off
from the Greenway is to the east.
name: Greenway

desc:
A trail weaves through the field primarily east to west. The trail forks to the
south towards a farm in the distance. To the north, is a steep cliff face that
could be climbed with some effort. The dusty curve of the Greenway is visible
nearby to the west.
name: Trail in a Field

desc:
The road enters a narrow gorge here. Tall cliffs to the east and west sandwich
the road allowing short hours of daylight. The ground beside the road is void of
plants and life except for some faint tracks of small lizards. The road
continues north and south.
name: Greenway

desc:
A tall cliff of dirt and stones rises in the west. The gorge to the north pauses
here and opens to the east into a field in front of some decrepit buildings.
Some grass and clover creep in from the east encroaching on the dusty road.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway moves in a large curve between the north and west. An imposing
cliff maintains a tall presence to the northwest. The rolling hills of the South
Downs can be glimpsed to the east through ruined buildings. A wide field sprawls
to the south.
name: Greenway

desc:
A steep hill rises west and north of the road, which travels east to curve
around it. A tree grows nearly in the centre of the road here, looking quite out
of place and creating an unusual obstacle that travellers must navigate around.
name: Greenway

desc:
Steep hills rise east and west of the grassy green road here, keeping it almost
continuously in shade. The earthen barriers muffle sound, keeping the air here
quiet. The ground must often stay wet, as deep wagon ruts can be seen following
the road north and south.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road is completely covered with dense, bright grass as it travels north and
south. Only the occasional footprint or cart track can be seen, showing this
part of the Greenway to be rarely travelled. Large, flat stones mark the start
of an old pathway branching off of the main road to the west.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road is hard to follow here as it curves from the west to the north. Some
time ago, sandy soil from a steep embankment to the east washed down and covered
the road, which was then overrun by sage and other brush.
name: Greenway

desc:
Large flat stones define a mostly overgrown path travelling west alongside an
ancient stone wall. To the east, the path joins up with a wider and more defined
road. Tall hills rise to the north, hindering travel in that direction. On
occasion, a breeze from the south brings with it a noxious odour originating
from some sort of flowering plant.
name: Stony Path

desc:
The remains of an ancient stone wall follow along the west and north sides of
the road as it curves and travels south and east. Thick, woody vines have grown
into the wall in such a way that they hold it in place. In places with no such
reinforcement, only rubble exists.
name: Ancient Wall along the Greenway

desc:
Game trails made by rabbits and deer criss-cross the grassy road here, leading
between the impassable bush thicket to the west and the open bushy plains to the
east. This part of the Greenway clearly sees more traffic from creatures with
four legs than from those with two. The road stretches as far as can be seen
north and south.
name: Greenway

desc:
Soft green grass hides most of the hoof and foot prints left by the scant few
travellers of this ancient road that travels north and south. A dense thicket of
thorny bushes encroaches on the road from the west. East of here, the bushy
plains are more open.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway bends to the north and west. The road is wide, impressive, and
lined with heavy stones. It is bordered on its sides by lush, green grasses and
low shrubs. A thick wall of brush runs parallel to the road on the eastern side.
This road is the primary route of travel between Bree and Tharbad.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The Greenway continues winding through the countryside, bending east and south.
It is bordered to the west by the base of a steep cliff, which runs parallel
with the road, and a line of dense brush to the east. The road itself is wide,
impressive, and lined with stones.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The Greenway narrows down here, as it passes between the base of a very steep
cliff to the west, and a wall of dense brush to the east. The road is lined with
heavy stones, which have been worn down in the centre due to heavy travel. The
sides of the road, between the cliff and brush, are bordered by lush, green
grasses.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway continues its journey between Bree and Tharbad here. To the west, a
vast, imposing thistle patch grows. The undergrowth is so thick in this
direction that it is only possible to travel in this direction on foot. Even
then, passage will be difficult and perilous.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway bends to the north and west here, continuing to wind through the
countryside between Bree and Tharbad. It is lined with heavy stones, and worn
well in the centre, a sign of heavy use and travel. Dense, thick brush border it
to the east.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway curves sharply east and south, running parallel to the base of a
steep cliff to the west. The walls of the cliff are densely covered in vines and
roots. The road is lined with stones, well-worn in its centre due to travel. To
the north, a dense thistle patch grows.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The Greenway continues its journey here between Bree and Tharbad. It narrows as
it passes between a steep, vine-covered cliff to the west, and a dense patch of
brush to the east. The road is lined with stones, and immediately bordered by
green grass.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road here is wide and open. There is a huge, imposing vine wall that blocks
passage to the west. It rises about twenty feet into the air and is covered in
long, sharp thorns. To the east, vast expansive fields stretch out and disappear
into the horizon.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road is majestic, wide, and well-trodden. It is bordered on either side by
verdant fields and rolling grasslands. The road itself is made of smooth,
well-worn stone, and it is a favoured route for travellers, merchants, and
soldiers alike.
name: Greenway

desc:
The ground is a patchwork of loose boulders and rocks. A brook babbles its way
through, its clear waters rushing over pebbles and rocks. A solitary hawthorn
tree stands guard near the brook. To the west, the land rises in rocky hilltops,
their jagged edges etch the horizon, forming a rugged skyline. To the east,
hilltops crouch under the weight of boulders. To the north, the land weaves a
junction of hilltops, where the hilltops meet in a convocation of stone and
earth.
name: Centre of a Stretch of Hills

desc:
This path, narrow yet steadfast, boasts a worn, weather-beaten surface. It lies
upon a bed of solid rock, etched out from the stony bluff. To the west, the land
rises with daunting, near-vertical sculptures of rock and scrub, far too steep
for any wanderer to traverse. To the east, the trail continues, turning,
twisting, and teetering across the hills. Southward, the trail beckons,
extending its reach toward the open fields beyond the Downs.
name: Trail through the Hillocks

desc:
Far removed from the Greenway, the trail here is hard to make out from the grass
and short brush. The path moves in a long arc between the west and north. The
area is a meadow sandwiched between two strands of hills of the South Downs.
name: Faint Trail

desc:
This section of the South Downs are a mix of grass-covered hills with large
patches of gravel and pebbles. The portions of the trail traversing the small
rocks show deep hoof marks where previous travellers struggled. The hills
continue to the southeast and northwest.
name: Gravelly Trail

desc:
The mounds of the South Downs are very close together here with little room for
level ground. The trail winds through the smoothest sections possible but would
be difficult for a wagon to follow closely. Short grass grows on the mounds with
frequent instances of tough bunches of weeds.
name: Jagged Trail

desc:
A few trees grow in a line, providing only sparse shade. Tall grass dominates
the landscape, resembling an unkempt pasture. A dusty trail runs straight east
to west, its surface cracked and dry from the sun. A stretch of the South Downs
hills can be seen close to the east.
name: Meandering Trail

desc:
The trail gets thinner the farther away from the Greenway it becomes. It climbs
between some hills to the south and meanders fairly straight to the east. Grass
encroaches on the trail as it appears rarely used. A cliff to the west descends
slowly to a field in the north.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
The trail winds around a large, low mound, connecting the western and northern
trail sections. Small pebbles from a dry streambed zig-zag down the side of the
wide mound, eventually cutting through the trail. The South Downs stretch out to
the south and east.
name: Curving Trail

desc:
The trail snakes west to east around low hills and knolls. A cliff to the north
prevents access. The hills continue far to the south and east as part of the
South Downs. There aren't any trees nearby but the small, occasional bushes and
patches of grass appear healthy and thriving.
name: Trail in Some Hills

desc:
An overgrown ridge, a forgotten scar of earth and stone, carves its way through
the grassland. Lichen and moss creep across its surface, a green and grey shroud
that softens its starkness. Staghorn clubmoss and reindeer lichen, common in
these boreal climes, claim dominion here, their forms creating a miniature
forest upon the ridge.
name: Overgrown Ridge

desc:
Dwarf willows and juniper shrubs cling to the crevices and crannies of this
rocky terrain, their roots delving into the stone, finding purchase in the most
unlikely places. A cluster of hillside daisies emerges from a crevice, their
delicate blooms creating a vibrant contrast amidst the monochromatic
surroundings. Amidst the boulders, a solitary Scots pine stands, with a massive
gnarled trunk and twisted branches.
name: Rocky Hilltops

desc:
This plateau is an unattainable expanse, its surface a rugged tapestry of
exposed rock and sporadic vegetation. Boulders, large and ominous, litter the
surface, their surfaces are worn and weathered from countless years of exposure.
Jutting crags of granite and basalt rise like jagged teeth from the plateau,
their stark forms casting long shadows down the slopes.
name: Plateau among the Hills

desc:
The ground here is a rugged tapestry, a mosaic of boulders and stones that cover
the little topsoil that remains. Large flat slabs of rock, remnants of some
ancient upheaval, lie strewn haphazardly about the slopes. A rocky outcrop, like
a skeletal hand reaching out from the earth, interrupts the gentle incline to
the west. To the east and north, the land rises sharply, forming steep, imposing
barriers that preclude any passage. A solitary trail, a sinewy ribbon of earth,
can be seen snaking its way in the south.
name: Depression among the Hills

desc:
A sloping ridge rises and stretches across the terrain. Amidst a rockface that
marks the crest of the ridge, verdant grass finds purchase. Beyond the ridge, a
line of the South Downs stretches towards the horizon. In the middle of the
ridge, a rocky outcrop stands proud and resolute. Its craggy face is a tableau
of shades of grey, from the lightest ash to the deepest slate.
name: Rocky Outcrop in a Sloping Ridge

desc:
The valley's verdant floor, carpeted with grass of an emerald hue, stretches
northward. To the west of the valley, bald hills rise in stark contrast, their
steep flanks bereft of the verdant cloak that adorns the ridge. To the south, a
rocky outcrop claims attention, a majestic formation that stands proudly on the
sloping ridge. The east, however, presents a formidable ascent forbidding
passage.
name: Valley Surrounded by Hills

desc:
In the heart of the valley, there stands an ancient tree, gnarled and twisted.
Its branches reach out as though trying to embrace the entirety of the valley.
Its roots are burrowed deep into the ground. At night, the valley is bathed in a
soft, ethereal glow, the moonlight casting long shadows, painting the landscape
in shades of silver and blue. The valley continues in all directions but east,
which is blocked by steep South Downs.
name: Ancient Tree in the Middle of a Wide Valley

desc:
Scattered around these hills, massive boulders cloaked in a shroud of vibrant
green moss, squat heavily on the earth. Great black oaks, the ancient lords of
the forest, loom ominously over the tall stones, their gnarled limbs stretch out
with dark and deeply furrowed bark. To the south, a worn trail meanders, winding
through the verdant hills of Arda. To the east, a rocky outcrop springs forth, a
sentinel of stone upon the sloping ridge. The northern vista is a tapestry of
rolling hillocks.
name: Hills Bordering a Trail

desc:
The terrain is adorned with undulating hillocks, rolling gracefully throughout
the landscape. Huge boulders are scattered across these hillocks, their shadows
cast silhouettes over the ground. The paths that wind their way through the
hillocks are not man-made but are etched into the land by the patient hand of
water. To the north, a cliff obstructs the path standing as a barrier.
name: Rolling Hillocks

desc:
The meadow seems to shy away from the towering slopes that guard the western and
southern frontiers. The grass, long and rich, is woven with intricate patterns
of wildflowers. Along the periphery, where the meadow surrenders to the valley,
a row of willows stands guard. Amidst this natural tapestry, a network of deep
animal tracks is etched into the soft black earth. The occasional burrow can be
spotted, a small, hidden sanctuary for the valley's smallest inhabitants.
name: Meadow in a Wide Valley

desc:
Swelling hillocks rise from the earth like the backs of great, slumbering
beasts. As a symphony of green, they are adorned with hardy weeds and resilient
grasses. Crabgrass stands in defiant clusters, its blades sharp and unyielding,
reaching up towards the sky amidst these grassy ranks, bearberry and wintergreen
creep, their low forms tracing the contours of the hillocks, their red berries a
stark contrast to the surrounding green.
name: Swelling Hillocks

desc:
The surface of the rock is rough and pitted. At the edge of the plateau, the
adventurers can see a series of rock outcrops that seem to defy gravity. These
formations jut out from the side of the promontory at impossible angles, as if
daring the wind to knock them down. Twisted, scraggly birches cling to the rocky
soil, their branches gnarled and twisted by the wind.
name: Steep, Rocky Hills

desc:
Gentle, undulating hills rise in the heart of these verdant grasslands. A soft
carpet of bright, green fescue graces their sinuous forms, each blade dancing in
the whispering wind. The grass grows taller along the sloping sides, with its
bladed heads weighed down by seeds, demonstrating the fertility of this land.
name: Verdant Knolls

desc:
The vibrant green meadow is like a sea of emeralds, stretching out in every
direction. The grass is soft and lush, perfect for grazing animals, and the air
is filled with the sweet fragrance of fresh-cut hay. Small wildflowers dot the
landscape, their bright colours providing a delightful contrast to the verdant
green of the grass.
name: Vibrant Green Meadow

desc:
This elevated meadow stands a touch above the surrounding plains, offering a
gentle rise to the landscape. The open area stretches endlessly in all
directions, its continuity sporadically interrupted by a boulder or a cluster of
stones. Situated at the heart of the meadow is a semi-circle of boulders,
assembled into a rudimentary windbreak. To the west of this area, the landscape
evolves dramatically, with steep, imposing cliffs.
name: Elevated Meadow

desc:
The vibrant green fields sprawl between the hills situated to the west, north
and east. The grass is soft and inviting, perfect for grazing sheep and cattle.
The air is filled with the sweet smell of fresh grass and wildflowers, and the
only sounds are the distant cries of birds and the gentle rustling of the leaves
in the breeze.
name: Green Pastures

desc:
The ground tumbles with mounds and knolls rising from a low level in the north
to sharp edges to the east and south. The drop-off is too precipitous to allow
movement to the east and south. Tall hills continue to the west. Hardy grass
covers the land with colourful flecks of wildflowers occasionally peeking
between the foliage.
name: Slanting Hills

desc:
Rolling hills progressively grow taller towards the west and south. Short grass
covers the ground up to the western and southern edges where reddish-brown dirt
is exposed on the steep cliff. The hill face to the south has natural landings
that could be climbed with enough skill.
name: Tall Hills

desc:
Steep escarpments mark the edges of these hills to the north and west, making
passage impossible in those directions. A narrow ridge passes onto the hills to
the south, while a gentle slope leads into a low valley to the east. Scattered
small rocks speckle the landscape, leaving occasional grey spots in the
otherwise green expanse.
name: Hilly Corner

desc:
Soft, rolling hills of the South Downs continue here from the west into the
south and east. A large patch of brush encroaches from the north and forms an
impenetrable barrier, blocking movement in that direction. The grass here
appears healthy from fertile soil and plentiful moisture.
name: South Downs

desc:
The area is lower than the surrounding land. A few tall trees have broken out of
the multitude of bushes and vines tangling the ground. Back to the east is the
only way out of the area as the other directions are too steep and overgrown
with vegetation to traverse.
name: Tangled Dell

desc:
The elevation of the grassy plains here seems to be slightly lower than the
surrounding fields causing water to slowly collect. The result is a lush meadow
where each imprint in the ground sends mud squishing out of the roots of the
grass.
name: Muddy Field

desc:
Hills to the north slowly lower down into this stretch of flat ground. Grass and
weeds grow in varying heights creating a scraggly landscape. Thin stalks of
brown vegetation sprout up between patches of grass and dip their heads in lazy
arcs. The fields sprawl out to the west and south with some knolls farther in
the distance.
name: Wild Field

desc:
The tall hills here follow a winding cliff to the east until smoothing out at a
lower elevation into a large field to the south. Eastern movement is blocked by
a natural wall made of large stones too difficult to climb over. The hills are
covered with dense, short grass.
name: Winding Cliff

desc:
These hills form the eastern part of a long section of the South Downs extending
to the northwest and the southeast. The mounds rise to the west until they end
in a cliff. The elevation slowly decreases into a field to the north but is
blocked to the east by a jumble of rocks. The large stones appear to have been
pushed upward out of the ground and tumbled back on top of themselves.
name: Patch of Knolls

desc:
The hills from the northwest climb higher here and stand on a precipice above
grasslands to the west and south. The ground is a mix of grass and pebbly soil.
A lone tree, long dead, stands on the edge of the cliff to the west. The bark
has been stripped and weathered, a grey trunk with broken limbs is all that
remains.
name: Hilly Overlook

desc:
Wild flowers of blues, reds, and whites dot the grassland. Perhaps the cliff in
the west provides the perfect amount of daily shade to spur the unusually large
number of blooms. Grass fills in every space where the flowers are not growing.
A valley curves between hills to the north and the field opens up substantially
in the east and south.
name: Flowering Meadow

desc:
The fertile field bends west to south as it bumps against the South Downs to the
north and east. While the valley continues to curve towards the northwest, a
larger field opens up to the south. An unusually high hill for the South Downs
is visible far off to the south, rising above the other mounds.
name: Winding Valley

desc:
Soft grass covers the ground from the bottom of cliffs to the southwest to the
cliffs farther away to the northeast. Animals traversing the space between the
hills of the South Downs have created a dirt path through the vegetation. The
path remains in the middle of the field as it angles along the hillside.
name: Winding Field

desc:
Knee-high grass flows from the west into this area and farther south. This
meadow is sandwiched between parallel strands of the South Downs. Rocky
outcroppings high above cut into the hills to the north and east. Some small
boulders have fallen out of the cliff and sit in the grass.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
The area looks as if nothing has ever stepped foot on this large field of
perfect green grass. Perhaps animal and other traffic prefers the trail winding
to the north and east. The soft grass may spring back to erase any footprints,
making it look like nothing has ever stepped foot on this large field of perfect
green grass.
name: Untouched Field

desc:
The soft grass is interrupted by a variety of dirt trails intersecting in the
middle of the area. Well-trod paths wind off in all directions except to the
south as a tall hill blocks the way. Animals travelling from grass fields
surrounding the South Downs seem to make regular use of this intersection.
name: Trails in a Field

desc:
A wide field of rough grass covers the area. Wildflowers dot the land adding
shades of colour to the sea of green. A difficult incline begins to the west.
Thick vines interlocking with each other in an impassable mess mark the
beginning of rough brush to the east and south.
name: Secluded Field

desc:
The only gentle slope on the hill is towards the east. All other directions are
precipitously steep. The top of the hill is craggy with rocks of many sizes
strewn unevenly. A few small burrows emerge from the dusty ground near the base
of larger boulders that give cover for small rodents.
name: Steep Hill

desc:
This quiet meadow is cradled between hills of the South Downs. The mounds stand
like sentinels, blocking passage to the north. But in other directions, they
rise more gently, allowing travellers to pass unimpeded. The field continues to
the east for a long distance. Patches of wildflowers speckle the otherwise
completely green landscape.
name: Quiet Meadow

desc:
The numerous, grass-covered hills continue here but bump up against a steeply
rising hill to the east. The slope is full of vegetation like the lower hills
but is impossible to climb. The area to the north and south begins to flatten
out while the South Downs continue in the west.
name: South Downs

desc:
Scraggly weeds patch the ground between round stones as large as a fist. The
ground beneath the stones is flat and does not appear saturated with stones. The
stones are strewn about the ground in a haphazard manner, as if they had been
tossed there by a giant hand. The hills of the South Downs surround on all sides
except the east.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
A grassy field stretches out, mostly flat but dotted with small holes.
Travellers should be careful not to twist an ankle in one of these holes, as
they are all exposed dirt and have not yet been filled with vegetation. To the
north, a tall hill blocks passage, while impenetrable brush blocks the east.
name: Pitted Field

desc:
This section of the South Downs becomes fairly narrow between a cliff to the
southwest and thick brush to the east. Some brush from the east has encroached
on ths hill with some thorny vines mingling in the thick grass. The hills
continue along the cliff edge to the west and south.
name: Curving Hill

desc:
The entire area is filled with bushes of varying heights and widths. Some bushes
hug the ground with vines curling out like stiff fingers. Other plants are as
large as a horse with thorny vines growing in tangled messes. While paths can be
made through most of the area, the brush is too dense to the east, north and
south.
name: Thorny Dead End

desc:
The hills of the South Downs reach a high point before the precipitous drop off
of the long cliff. Fields stretch out for many miles below the cliff to the
west. From this height, brush can be seen encroaching on the hills as they
continue to the southeast.
name: High Overlook

desc:
The flowing hills of the South Downs narrow here between fields to the north and
a cliff edge to the south. The hills continue to the east and west. A large
expanse of fields stretches out beyond the cliff. Some broad-leafed bushes
cluster together in the creases between the knolls.
name: Waning Hills

desc:
Scattered short bushes and tall grasses thin out towards the cliff edge to the
south and west. Large and small rocks cover the ground near the dropoff and
prevent vegetation from taking hold. It would be perilous to attempt to climb
down the cliff.
name: Cliff Edge

desc:
Undulating fields of grass and scrubby bushes cover the area. The cliff
following the edge of the South Downs is just to the southwest and a valley
extends directly east. Peaceful, green hills continue in all other directions.
Some bare rocks are visible closer to the cliff.
name: South Downs

desc:
The grassy hills of the South Downs begin to descend down to the east where they
meet a long field. Hills continue in all other directions without any unique
features. A few solitary trees break up the green landscape. Thick bark covers
the trunks leading to sparse branches with wide, floppy leaves.
name: South Downs

desc:
Knee-high grasses sprout from the fertile soil. Only a few solitary trees take
advantage of the nutrient-dense ground, but they have grown tall and are full of
leaves. A protected valley is to the east and all other directions continue with
smooth hills.
name: South Downs

desc:
The lush green hills of the South Downs are a little more brown in this area on
account of many scattered, small pebbles. The stones are sprinkled around like
remnants of large boulders being smashed into tiny pieces. Hills surround the
area in all directions.
name: South Downs

desc:
It is clear this is the eastern edge of a section of the South Downs as numerous
hills curve to the south and west. A large field expands to the north east
before another range of hills takes shape. Some large boulders taller than a man
collect in the east edge of the area.
name: South Downs

desc:
The hills here are dominated by one that is significantly taller than the
others. Steep sides fall from the peak in all directions except a small path to
the west. The height creates a spectacular view of the fields to the east and
miles of the South Downs to the west.
name: Lofty Hill

desc:
The hills of the South Downs extend in all directions like the undulating sand
dunes of a desert. The green hills of grass rise and fall gracefully without
sharp edges. Animal droppings dot the landscape frequently on account of regular
visits by grazing creatures.
name: South Downs

desc:
Rolling hills extend in all directions for several miles. Green waves of grass
rise and fall gently, while occasional trees break up the sea of emerald. A
rabbit hole is unusually obvious being the only speck of brown in the lush
landscape.
name: South Downs

desc:
The ground rises from the valley in the west to meet the rest of the hills of
the South Downs. Small, coffin-shaped lumps push up in modest hillocks around
the larger hills. Scrubby weeds mingle with wildflowers in an otherwise grassy
landscape.
name: Ascending Hills

desc:
A valley stretches west to east, sandwiched between cliffs of the South Downs.
The grass is long and falls lazily onto itself creating a pillowy sea of green.
A faint, narrow trail can be made out, snaking through the valley where animals
traverse between the hills rising at both ends.
name: Verdant Valley between Some Hills

desc:
The hills of the South Downs come to an abrupt end here at the western boundary.
A shadowy set of ruined buildings sit below the edge of the cliff. There is a
narrow valley to the east between miles of flowing hills. The grass covering the
ground is soft and short.
name: Cliff above the Ruins

desc:
Fields to the west begin rising slowly here as a soft entry to the rolling hills
of the South Downs. Almost treeless grassy knolls extend for miles to the east
and south. The ground between the blades of grass is dark and fertile. A large
farm can be seen to the north.
name: Rising Hill

desc:
Rolling hills of grass tall enough to hide a rabbit cover the area. In the dips
at the lowest place between hills there are some snaking bushes enjoying the
darkest space available in the lush knolls. The vegetation in the shadows seems
to be doing well, but it still gets some sunlight over the low hills.
name: South Downs

desc:
The highest point on the hills allows for a view over the farm to the west. To
the north, glimpses of a trail curving around hills can be seen traversing west
to east. The hills are covered with lush green grass with a handful of trees
spaced far apart.
name: South Downs

desc:
Low hills are spread out with wide tracks of level ground roaming between them.
Dark soil is occasionally interrupted by sparse bushes and a few isolated trees.
A wagon track can be made out angling on the flattest earth towards a gate in
the east.
name: Hills before a Gate

desc:
A long, deep ditch hugs the Greenway to the north and west. It is too deep to
enter and exit safely. Exposed rocks bulge out of the sides of the ditch but are
not visible anywhere else in the shaggy fields nearby. A farm is visible to the
east, perched on a strand of the South Downs.
name: Deep Ditch

desc:
This peaceful field has access to the Greenway to the west but is dominated by a
view of crumbling and shady buildings to the south. Some square and chiselled
stones obviously used in buildings are scattered around the area, becoming more
numerous farther south. The South Downs gain height to the east.
name: Field in Front of the Ruins

desc:
The cliff here curves slowly in a long arc along the west and south edges. The
ground is mostly exposed rock with no clear seams. Grass begins to grow farther
away from the edge of the cliff. The Greenway can be seen several miles to the
east.
name: Rounded Cliff

desc:
The grassy hills are like frozen ocean waves with troughs and crests connected
by long, smooth lines. A few old trees with sturdy, brown trunks root into the
dirt. A cliff is not far off to the southwest. A low valley unusually absent of
hills stretches to the east.
name: South Downs

desc:
Ample fields of grass mixed with a dense, spongy moss blanket the area and seem
to deaden the sounds of nature. The moss appears most dense around some small
dark holes in the centre of the valley. Moist air occasionally wafts out of the
darkness.
name: Mossy Valley

desc:
A stream of water sprouts out of the side of one of the grassy knolls, flowing a
short distance before collecting in a small pond. The water flowing down the
hill would not cover even the first knuckle of a finger penetrating the surface.
The pond is no more than a foot deep and is crystal clear, unadulterated by
muddy disturbances.
name: Old Spring

desc:
Vines and tangly bushes curl up from the edge of the cliff like a man clutching
for his life. While the ground below is covered in thick brush, it only covers a
short distance from the precipice. The rest of the ground is mostly grass and
loose stones.
name: Bushy Cliff

desc:
A peaceful field of knee-high grass is fenced in by piles of stones. This would
be an ideal place for a flock of sheep or cows to graze. The only way out around
the lengths of assembled stones is to the north. The ground is very flat. A
small grove of trees give cover in the southeast corner.
name: Field near the Ruins

desc:
The Greenway lies to the west, down a gentle slope. Rolling hills spread out to
the south, while flatter ground is visible east. The tall grass covering the
knolls is disturbed closer to the Greenway. Perhaps travellers have favored the
area with matted vegetation as a place to relax while watching the road.
name: Crowded Knolls

desc:
A large jumble of jagged rocks slowly climbs to a height above nearby trees. It
is much wider than it is tall, but a difficult slope to the west prevents
movement down to the Greenway. Some of the jutting rocks have sharp corners and
are as tall as a man. Small sharp stones chipped from their larger brethren are
common on the ground.
name: Rocky Hill

desc:
The hills here rise and fall like lazy waves, never reaching more than a man's
height. The ground is primarily pebbles no larger than a thumb. Some of the
stones have sharp edges that could almost slice skin while others are smooth as
river rock. Some blades of grass have managed to split through the piles of
stones and show signs of life.
name: Pebble Hills

desc:
The ground dips very slightly but consistently into a large bowl shape between
the collection of knolls to the southwest and the South Downs to the northeast.
The centre and lowest part of the bowl contains a small area of dried, rotting
vegetation indicating frequent standing water.
name: Sunken Field

desc:
The vegetation here grows thick and dense, undisturbed as it is naturally
sheltered on almost all sides by natural and man-made obstacles. Some ruined
buildings and crumbling walls stand to the north while a stone fence runs along
the western edge of the area. The South Downs to the east end abruptly in a tall
cliff. Some of the grass here has been flattened apparently by animals bedding
down.
name: Sheltered Field

desc:
This area is dominated by the remnants of a landslide from the cliff to the
northeast. The cliff remains tall, as if a shelf of dirt sheared off and flowed
down into the valley between the South Downs and the grouping of hills to the
west. Some bushes and grass have started to push up through the dirt that was
once part of the cliff, but the area is still mostly exposed soil.
name: Landslide Debris

desc:
The steep cliff against the South Downs to the north and east is covered with
scraggly bushes and leafy vines. The most dense area is where the flat ground
meets the bottom of the precipice. The vegetation opens up slightly farther
towards the surrounding fields.
name: Bushy Grove

desc:
Species of tall shrubs and brambles seem to thrive here in the shade offered by
the cliff to the north. The brush opens a little towards the south as the
terrain opens up into huge fields. Some fallen trees are scattered around the
area, mostly overgrown with the wild brush.
name: Protected Brush

desc:
Stones in the cliff the colour of dried algae indicate a stream once ran over
the edge of the hills into the area. A trail of small, round pebbles traces the
path of the water that once flowed here. It appears to have flowed west through
the vegetation to lower ground.
name: Old Streambed

desc:
The grass here mixes with large swathes of wild barley and wheat. The golden
brown of the farm crops droop over in tall curls, like the hair of a sleeping
giant. Perhaps a seed shipment on the Greenway was waylaid and some of the cargo
ended up being scattered here. The wind whispers through the stalks, creating a
gentle rustling sound.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The cliffs to the north cast long shadows over the vegetation. A variety of
rocks from the size of a fingernail up to the size of a head of cabbage mix in
with the grasses and short bushes. The highest point in this area is where some
dirt is sloped up against the cliff.
name: Shadowy Plains

desc:
Among the green grass of the field is a vague footpath to a long-abandoned
building. The roof has caved in and the walls are only partially standing in
sections. There are long, sturdy wooden benches strewn around the cobblestone
floor. Fresh dirt has been tracked in from the faint path inside the old
building and lead to one of the overturned benches.
name: Ruined Building

desc:
Numerous pebbles are scattered over the entire area allowing only solitary
blades of grass to push up between the small stones. The rocks are jagged and
almost sharp on some edges as if a cliff of the South Downs was shattered here
into thousands of tiny pieces.
name: Field of Pebbles

desc:
The area is largely covered by soft grass. Small, willowy bushes spot the
landscape providing ample area to move around them. A larger patch of bushes
crowd next to the cliff of the South Downs to the east. Stiff bushes crowd in an
impossible wall to the south.
name: Field of Small Bushes

desc:
Along the edges of the grassland, the hillsides are covered in entangled
shrubbery and sharp-edged boulders that make for treacherous footing. The fields
are lush with vibrant green grasses swaying gently in the breeze. In the
distance to the west, a ridge of hills can be seen rising against the horizon.
Flocks of birds soar overhead, their wings catching the wind as they ride the
currents.
name: Scrub-covered Flatlands

desc:
Several large patches of ground are exposed with no plant life. Animal
footprints have been stamped into the hard ground with no clear indication of
how old they are. Sparse grass fills in the area between the spots of open
ground.
name: Dirty Field

desc:
Grass here is mixed with stiff weeds crawling over the dirt. Some taller patches
of weeds sprout up much higher than the surrounding vegetation. Fields stretch
out in all directions for miles. A cliff following the edge of the South Downs
is visible far off to the north east.
name: Wild Field

desc:
A long ridge of hills to the south and east bulge up in this area, sheltering a
secluded meadow. Birds have made their home in some small natural caves high
above the ground in the cliff wall. A handful of trees stand away from the steep
terrain.
name: Sheltered Meadow

desc:
Thick brush to the north gives way to knee-high grass here and in the
surrounding area. Small lumps can be felt in the dirt but the ground is mostly
flat in all directions. A tall cliff of the South Downs can be seen off to the
north east.
name: Unobstructed Plains

desc:
The land here is almost completely flat and unremarkable. Hills are visible to
south and northwest but this area is void of any notable change in altitude.
Lush grass, ideal for grazing cattle, flows seamlessly on dirt that is almost
black.
name: Sweeping Plains

desc:
The land bows down slightly in a long arc between the hills in the north and
south. Long fields continue to the east. The lush grass in the fields shows
evidence of grazing, which suggests that animals may have been avoiding the more
difficult terrain of the hills.
name: Low Valley

desc:
While the land is covered in thick, shaggy grass, the bushes to the west have
encroached. Seeds blown in the wind from the nearby brush have deposited in the
area and taken hold with little challenge as they edge out the softer grass.
Some large jagged rocks stand in the north.
name: Bushy Field

desc:
A maze of trails from rabbits, deer, and their canine foes winds its way through
the brush. These trails can be followed in any direction. The flat, curving
Greenway wrap around to the north and west, while clearer plains open to the
east. The brushy maze continues to the south.
name: Maze through Some Brush

desc:
The peaceful slope to the west becomes a more rocky set of hills here. It is as
if something punched up a fistful of rocks and boulders from under the surface
of the earth. The jumble of jagged stones makes a wall to the east preventing
movement.
name: Rocky Hill

desc:
A single, long hill starts sloping up from west to east where it meets other
knolls and crags that extend for several miles towards the southeast. Peaceful
fields extend in all directions except to the east. Soft grass covers the ground
completely.
name: Sloping Hill

desc:
Thick bushes from the south and west invade the area but with much less density
and height. Thorny vines tunnelling through the earth create frequent tripping
hazards. This vegetation shares the space with sparse grass and many large
patches of dirt.
name: Scraggly Field

desc:
The air holds the smell of sage and other herbs. Thick bushes stand tall here,
providing shade during the day. To the south they become more dense and
completely block passage. The brush opens up for easier travel to the east and
west.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
This rugged terrain has been overgrown by vegetation with rocky crags emerging
from this field of grass. Occasional stands of trees are dotting the landscape,
providing shelter for smaller creatures, their trunks sturdy and branches
reaching high into the sky. They seem to stand as sentinels, guarding the
peaceful fields below. The air is filled with the scent of dry earth and dusty
stone, mingled with the sharp tang of the brush from the south.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
Boulders extend out of the earth and create a maze of outcroppings and craggy
peaks, making the shadows of the large rocks loom over the area. The foothills
can be quite steep in places, with sheer cliffs and rocky outcroppings that make
climbing difficult. Deeper into the foothills, cliffs obstruct the path forward.
The sound of trickling water can be heard echoing through the foothills, adding
to the tranquil atmosphere of the region.
name: Overgrown Foothills

desc:
The slope of the land becomes steeper, leading down into a deep valley that lies
at the centre of the woody hill range. Water has worn paths into the hills
around this area, forming shallow streams and gullies that twist and turn
throughout the terrain. Along the washed-out trails, a hardy brush grows,
clinging tenaciously to the rocky soil. Its leaves are tough and leathery, and
its branches twist and turn like the gnarled fingers of an old crone. To the
east, the hills seem to be covered in patches of verdant grass, while to the
west, a rocky outcrop dominates the landscape.
name: Deep Valley in the Woody Hills

desc:
A vast expanse of grasslands stretches out as far as the eye can see. The grass,
tall and golden, sways gently in the breeze, forming rippling waves. The air is
thick with the sweet scent of blooming flowers, and the soft rustling of the
reeds is calming background noise.
name: Grasslands near Foothills

desc:
The fields begin to change, becoming more rocky and rugged as the ridge narrows.
The land slopes downward abruptly towards the west, the gently rolling hills
giving way to a steep drop-off that leads into a hollow in the landscape. The
view from the edge of the grassland is breathtaking, as the horizon seems to
stretch out forever, with only the foothills in the distance breaking the line
of sight. The ground is littered with boulders and small rock formations, while
steep ravines and gullies cut through the hillsides.
name: Gentle Fields along a Steep Ridge

desc:
Waterfalls cascade down the cliffsides, creating pools of crystal-clear water. A
thick seam of peat runs through the centre of the hills. This peat seam gives
the ground a soft and spongy texture, making it difficult to traverse on foot.
The outcrop on the western side of the foothills rises abruptly from the ground,
its bald, steep sides stretching towards the western side of the valley. The
rock is rough and jagged, with sharp chips of stone littering the ground around
it.
name: Peat-covered Promontory

desc:
The stony paths become steeper and more treacherous, and the boulders loom ever
larger in their path. These cliffs are made of rugged rock faces that have been
weathered by centuries of wind and rain. Into the hills, more rocky outcroppings
and cliffs can be seen. Some of these formations are so immense that they dwarf
even the tallest trees in the region.
name: Pass between the Ridge of Hills

desc:
The curving walls of this cave resemble a large egg. The air is damp although no
water source can be seen. Some water droplets slowly drip down exposed rocks in
the sides of the cave. Old bones with teeth and claw marks blend with the
decaying corpses of small animals.
name: Smelly Cave

desc:
The hills of the South Downs slope down to the south into an area full of wild
plants and vegetation. A sharp cliff is to the west and a wide chasm prevents
movement to the east. The sharp trough between hills is unusual in the soft,
rolling hills of the South Downs. It is too wide to leap across and at least
thirty feet deep.
name: South Downs

desc:
The field is overrun by thick flowery brambles, towering overgrown grass, and
patches of lichen that cling to every surface. Shrubs of blackberries grow
around the thickets. The brambles intertwine with one another, creating a
natural barrier that no man could penetrate without great effort.
name: Thick Flowery Brambles

desc:
The thickets seem to form an impenetrable wall, daring any who might venture to
pass through their thorny barrier. Even the bravest of souls might find
themselves lost within the maze of brush, their progress slowed to a crawl by
the tangled web before them. The thorny thickets seem to come alive, writhing
and reaching out, trying to grasp at any who passed by. Amidst the tangled maze,
a small walkway emerges, crossing over a gushing stream. The pathway is narrow,
and made of rough-hewn stones, barely visible among the lush growth.
name: Old Thickets

desc:
Some robust trees with bushy leaves populate the area. The trees are spread out,
prefering isolation over the community of a grove. Short grass mixed with weeds
prevents almost any glimpse of dirt or rocks. A long abandoned broken wagon
wheel is growing moss, propped against one of the trees.
name: Field by the Greenway

desc:
This is a precipice on the eastern edge of a rugged, hilly region that rolls out
west and north of here. A slope drops off to the east, steep but traversable to
flat plains beyond. In the distance, further east, the flat Greenway winds its
way through a valley.
name: Edge of a Hilly Region

desc:
Three long trenches run from west to east as if carved by the claws of an
unimaginable beast . Each trench is higher than the last, eventually culminating
in a ridge preventing passage to the north. The bottoms of the long cuts contain
a permanent mud. Some tough weeds struggle to grow on the steep sides of the
troughs.
name: Slight Ridge

desc:
The South Downs come to a point here, poking toward the Greenway to the east.
Down the slopes to the north, a large stand of trees covers the horizon, the
only significant amount of trees for many miles. A narrow ridge across the
mounds leads west, deeper into the Downs.
name: Downs along the Greenway

desc:
The forest is thin here, encroached by the rolling downs to the west and the
Greenway to the east. A steep, rocky slope to the west makes travel that way
impossible, but to the south, the slopes are much more forgiving. The Greenway
bends around the forest to the east, giving a snake-like switchback to the
otherwise straight road.
name: Edge of a Small Forest

desc:
The undergrowth thins here, giving way to a crunchy layer of grass, branches,
and brown leaves. The trees grow high, with very few branches except at the top
of the canopy. A few spots of sky are sprinkled across the treetops, giving a
speckled look to the view up above.
name: Forested Glade

desc:
The eastern end of this stand of trees butts up to the Greenway here. A few of
the trees have been cut, evidently needed by some industrious traveller. To the
west, the forest thickens as the trees grow taller and closer together. To the
south, the Greenway slowly snakes its way around the hills to the southwest.
name: Patch of Forest beside the Greenway

desc:
A little-used path marked by flat stones rises up to greet steep banks to the
north and west. The path leads down a gentle hill to the east and south, closely
following a stone wall that cuts its way across the green landscape.
name: Path along Some Hills

desc:
The remains of an ancient stone wall cut across the landscape, running southwest
to northeast. Thick, woody vines have grown into the wall in such a way that
they hold it in place. In places with no such reinforcement, only rubble exists.
A path defined by wide flat stones follows the wall, leading north and west.
name: Path Following an Ancient Wall

desc:
Large flat stones define a mostly overgrown path travelling east and south
alongside an ancient stone wall. A shallow ditch to the north is filled with
tall, dense grasses whose tassels rise ten feet or more into the air.
name: Stony Path near Tall Grasses

desc:
Tall wheat grows in this once prosperous field, but it does not look like it has
been harvested in many years. A shallow ditch curves around the field from the
north to the west. Dense, tall grasses fill the ditch, blocking travel. South
and east of here, a trail and a stone wall travel across the land.
name: Abandoned Wheat Field

desc:
Tall, dense grasses rise in all directions. Their tassels stretch ten feet or
more into the air. The air is filled with the humming of insects. A faint trail
makes its way northward, hard packed gravel that has barely kept from being
overrun by vegetation over the decades.
name: Tall Grasses

desc:
Stone foundations are all that remain of several structures on either side of a
stony path. This may have once been a large farmstead or a small village, but
anything of value is long gone. Southeast of here can be seen a stone wall
crossing the plains. The path loosely follows it to the east and south. A
cluster of trees to the west provides shade, but blocks travel in that
direction.
name: Path between Ruined Buildings

desc:
A path marked by wide, flat stones set into the ground meanders its way north
and west through the plains. A decrepit stone wall crosses the plains to the
south, running southwest to northeast. The plains around here look like they may
once have been cultivated farmland, but untended fields and the occasional
building foundation are all that remain.
name: Path near a Wall

desc:
A stony path narrows as it passes between a thick copse of pine trees to the
northwest, and a moss-covered stone wall to the southeast. Not far west of here,
the wall ends abruptly. Sound is dampened by the barriers, and the air holds the
bright scent of pine and the musty smell of rotting plant matter.
name: Narrow, Stony Path

desc:
Stone cairns standing no more than two feet tall periodically mark a thin trail
travelling through the plains. A pile of rotting wooden debris lies near the
trail, the remains of a hovel that must have collapsed decades ago. The trail
continues east and west from here. A dense thicket of pine trees blocks travel
and visibility to the north, while to the south the scrubby plains open up for
miles.
name: Overgrown Trail

desc:
A dense thicket of pine trees stands to the north, wrapping around to the west.
In front of the thicket west of here, a hundred yards from the trail, several of
the trees were harvested and stacked into a simple log structure standing about
four feet high. Round stones and crude masonry are pressed between the logs of
the walls, providing structure and adornment. The roof is topped with gravel and
earth, upon which grow many ferns.
name: Trail near a Short Log Structure

desc:
A dusty, stony trail bends and stretches westward and northward. It is
surrounded by a lush, green grassland. Patches of tall, thick weeds and low,
scraggly shrubs dot the landscape. Far to the south, a rising, thorny scrubland
dominates the horizon.
name: Stony Trail

desc:
The northern portion of this large thistle patch is filled with low brush and
dry vegetation. Some thorny vines hang down in some areas, but become much more
prevalent to the south. Patches of grassy ground appear here and there, with no
tall vegetation growing in these openings. Elsewhere, low shrubs and young, thin
trees can be found all about the area.
name: Northern Edge of a Large Thistle Patch

desc:
Creeping thistle weeds dominate the landscape, with blue flowers sprouting from
the tops. Dry, thin brush and low shrubs grow all about, with the blue flower
heads protruding through occasionally. The ground is mostly covered in grass,
with bare patches popping up every now and then. Patches of dense, low, green
shrubbery make rare appearances in the otherwise dry landscape.
name: Northern End of a Large Thistle Patch

desc:
The terrain toward the east drops abruptly over a steep cliff, from which the
Greenway can be spotted below. Dense, thin, and scraggly brush grows abundantly
everywhere but to the east, where it thins before eventually opening before the
cliff's edge. The occasional blue thistle flower sprouts up sporadically.
name: In a Thistle Patch

desc:
An ancient stone wall northwest of here cuts across a gently rising hill. Tall
grasses bend back and forth when there is a breeze, creating waves across the
landscape. Several thistles with prickly leaves grow here, becoming more dense
to the east and south.
name: Ancient Stone Wall Lining a Thistle Patch

desc:
Thorny bushes interspersed with prickly thistles grow in abundance here,
becoming impassibly thick to the east and south. The dense brush continues to
the north, but thins out westward. The sounds of insects hum constantly, but
there are no other signs of wildlife.
name: Brushy Thistle Patch

desc:
An ancient stone wall cuts across the the brush covered landscape northwest of
here. To the east, a tall, dense thicket of stems and thorns makes travel that
direction impossible. It meets up with the stone wall directly to the north,
creating an abrupt corner. Several thistles with sharp leaves and blue flowers
grow in the open.
name: Walls of Stone and Thorns Surrounding a Thistle Patch

desc:
Waist-high vegetation dominates the landscape, making normal movement through
the area more difficult. Dense, thick, thorny vines hang down all around as
well, adding to the difficulty of moving safely and easily about. Large areas of
grassy ground, devoid of the dangerous vegetation, appear occasionally. Blue
thistle flowers can be found scattered about, in no consistent manner of growth.
name: In a Thistle Patch

desc:
Thin, dry, scraggly brush grows high and dense, with vines hanging down from
above all throughout. Creeping thistle weeds grow all over, their blue heads
adding dots of colour to the otherwise dull landscape. There is an occasional
grassy area, devoid of brush, which provides some reprieve from tedious travel.
name: In a Thistle Patch

desc:
A steep cliff overlooking the Greenway drops off to the east. There is a deep,
muddy hole that has been formed here, which has turned into a dark, putrid, and
stagnant pond. Bullfrogs croak and splash into the pond every now and then.
Dense, but thin vegetation grows here, with vines hanging down. Blue thistle
flowers grow all around.
name: In a Thistle Patch

desc:
Thin, dry brush and vegetation grow high here. Thorny vines hang down all
around. The dry, cracked ground is covered in a blanket of thin, but
densely-growing roots. Blue thistle flowers grow all over the landscape, and
travel here is relatively easy.
name: Thistle-covered Brush

desc:
Tree lines have formed here in the north and the south. There is a
peculiar-looking decayed tree that stands out from the rest of the in the thin
line to the north. Otherwise, bushy vegetation grows all around, but thins to
the east as it approaches the Greenway. Blue thistle flowers dot the
surroundings, breaking up the monotonous terrain with splashes of colour.
name: Stand of Trees

desc:
The hollow trunk of the rotten tree opens up into a surprisingly vast space.
There are thousands of tiny holes in the bark walls, with some small creatures
moving throughout them. Scraping and scratching can be heard throughout,
emanating from within the walls. There is a large hole in the northern wall.
name: Inside a Tree

desc:
Inside this opening in a tree, there are thousands of tiny holes in the walls.
It seems as though this is some sort of a nest for some small creatures who can
move inside these holes. On the ground, there are a lot of small white eggs.
This space is so tight and cramped, it is almost impossible to manoeuvre around.
name: Hole in a Tree

desc:
A steep drop-off over a cliff edge impedes travel to the east, below which the
Greenway can be spotted. The ground here is covered in thick roots and vines,
making travel very tedious and perilous. An impassable wall of vines has formed
to the west, and blue thistle flowers grow all about.
name: In a Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
The terrain rises here, forming a steep drop-off over a cliff to the south and
east. Entangled vines creep in from the south, forming a layer of dense vines
and roots on the ground, which are covered in sharp thorns and spines. The
vegetation is dense and dangerous.
name: Deep inside a Thistle Patch

desc:
Dense, dangerous vegetation make travel here perilous. Adding to the peril, the
terrain rises to the south and ends at a steep drop-off over a cliff edge. A
blanket of vines encroaches in from the south, forming a carpet of vines and
roots that are covered in thorns and spines.
name: In a Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
The heart of the thistle patch is filled with dense, dry vegetation, making
movement here extremely difficult in any direction. Thorny, spiny vines hang
from above, and seem to sway and shift on their own accord. Blue thistle flowers
grow abundantly here, and the ground is covered in a very thick, dense carpet of
roots, which seem to cover an opening below.
name: Heart of a Thistle Patch

desc:
Dense vegetation can be seen in every direction, and almost everything seems to
be covered in sharp thorns and spines. Thick roots carpet the ground, and rise
up to form large walls blocking passage to the west and south. Blue thistle
flowers grow abundantly here.
name: In a Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
Thin, dry, scraggly vegetation grows here at the edge of a thistle patch. It
grows thicker into the thistle patch to the south, with blue thistle flowers
scattered amongst it. To the north, the ground flattens out into a green
grassland.
name: Edge of a Thistle Patch

desc:
Dense, dry, scraggly, and treacherous vegetation grows here, deep inside a
thistle patch. Everything seems to be covered in sharp thorns and long spines.
The ground is dry and dusty, with no grass covering the bare dirt. Thistle
flowers grow abundantly all around.
name: Thorny Thistle Patch

desc:
Blue thistle flowers grow abundantly all around in the dense, treacherous
vegetation here. A tall wall of dense, thorny vines has formed to the west,
blocking any hopes of passage in that direction. The ground is dry, cracked, and
dusty. Overhead, a canopy of tangled, thick vines stretches in every direction.
name: Prickly Thistles

desc:
The green, lush grassland to the north transitions into the outskirts of a
thistle patch here. The vegetation is thin, but grows thicker to the south. The
landscape here is dotted with blue thistle flowers, growing on long, thorny
green stems.
name: Thin Brush

desc:
A blanket of thick, thorny vines has formed a cover overhead, allowing sunlight
to still shine through during the daytime. The vegetation here is dangerous,
thorny, dry, and tangled. The ground is dry, dusty, and worn to the bare dirt.
Blue thistle flowers grow all around, spread throughout the dangerous
surrounding vegetation.
name: Deep within a Thistle Patch

desc:
A tangled canopy of dry, thorny vines stretches out overhead in all directions.
The landscape is dominated by dense, thorny brush and travel is difficult to the
west and south, and impossible to the north and east. The atmosphere is dry and
dusty. Blue thistle flowers grow all throughout the dense brush.
name: Deep within a Thistle Patch

desc:
Overhead, the canopy of tangled, thorny vines continues to stretch out in all
directions, ascending higher into the air toward the south and southwest. The
vegetation is dry and thorny. Almost everything here is covered in thorns. The
ground is dry, cracked, and dusty.
name: Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
A towering wall of green vines, thickly-covered in long, sharp thorns, dominates
the south here. Passage and sight beyond the wall to the south is impossible. At
the base of the wall, thick roots grow and tangle together in the ground, where
they disappear into the dry, cracked dirt. Blue thistle flowers grow all
throughout the wall and the surrounding brush.
name: Along a Huge Wall of Vines

desc:
A huge, towering wall of thick, thorny vines bends to the north and east. The
wall towers at least twenty feet into the air, blocking passage in either
direction. From the top of the massive wall, a thick canopy of thorny vines
extends out to the north and east.
name: Corner of a Wall of Vines

desc:
A natural corridor has been formed here between a steep cliff wall to the north
and a blockade of thorns to the south. The wall is formed from thick, green
vines, covered in thorns, and woven tightly together. The wall rises
approximately twenty feet high, and a canopy stretches from the top of it toward
the top of the cliff to the north. The vegetation here is dense and treacherous.
name: Brush-filled Corridor

desc:
There is a steep cliff wall to the north and a wall of thorns to the south and
east. A canopy forms from the top of the wall and stretches northward to the
cliff and climbs to its crest. The vegetation is dense and makes manoeuvring
difficult. The thorny vegetation here shifts restlessly, seemingly of its own
accord, and the ground is covered in a carpet of tangled roots.
name: Brush-filled Dead-end

desc:
A huge, towering wall of green, thorny, entangling vines has grown here,
blocking passage to the south. It stands approximately twenty feet tall, and a
dense canopy grows from it, stretching northerly. Other dry, thorny vegetation
grows abundantly and treacherously, and blue thistle flowers dominate the
surroundings.
name: Along a Wall of Vines and Thorns

desc:
Dense brush and thorny, spiny bushes dominate the landscape, and a canopy of
entangled vines grows overhead. The vegetation grows thinner below. The ground
is dry and cracked, with not much grass covering the bare dirt. Blue thistles
grow plentifully.
name: Deep within a Thistle Patch

desc:
The treacherous vegetation grows thick in all directions and becomes almost
impassable. Navigating and manoeuvring here are extremely difficult.
Blue-flowered thistle plants grow sporadically throughout the briars and
brambles. The atmosphere is dry and dusty.
name: Deep within a Thistle Patch

desc:
Brush, thorns, briars, and brambles grow thick and dense here, rising up about
eight feet from the ground. The ground beneath the thick vegetation is bare,
with small patches of thin grasses here and there. The air is dry and dusty. All
about, thistle plants with blue flowers grow randomly.
name: Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
Brush, thorns, briars, and brambles dominate the landscape here. To the north,
they are much thinner and eventually come to an end. To the south and east, the
daunting vegetation thickens and becomes denser, making passage in those
directions much more difficult. Thistle plants with blue flowers grow randomly
among the vegetation.
name: Edge of a Dense Thistle Patch

desc:
The grasslands to the north quickly transition into a landscape of thinning
vegetation. Vines, thorns, and brush grow thicker southward. To the east, a
dense, impassable wall of thorny vines and shrubbery has formed. Blue thistle
flowers grow abundantly here throughout the brush.
name: Edge of a Thistle Patch

desc:
A stony trail travels westward and northward here. It is worn in the centre,
providing evidence that it is well-travelled. Loose gravel and pebbles line the
sides. A lush, green grassland surrounds it, stretching out in every direction,
with the occasional patch of thick weeds and shrubs sprouting up randomly.
name: Stony Trail

desc:
A sea of lush, green grass stretches in every direction. Spanning east and west,
a well-worn, stony path has been cut into the ground. The path is composed of
small, loose pebbles and gravel, with the ground worn bare in the centre.
name: Stony Trail

desc:
A flat, expansive plain stretches out in all directions. The ground is covered
in lush, green grass. Every now and then, small patches of thin shrubs and
scraggly trees break up the monotony of the plain. Occasional patches of small
stones and gravel appear sporadically as well.
name: Flat Plain

desc:
A wide, stony trail bends south and west here. The centre of the trail is worn
all the way to the bare dirt. Beneath it, the centre is surrounded by loose
gravel on each side. Surrounding the trail is a rich, lush green field that
stretches in all directions. Low bushes and shrubs grow waist-high on the
borders of the trail.
name: Stony Trail

desc:
A wide, stony trail spans east and west. Small stones and loose gravel surround
a well-travelled and worn-down centre, which is smooth and bare. It is enveloped
in every direction by a lush green grassland. Patches of tall weeds and low
shrubs dot the landscape.
name: Stony Trail

desc:
A thick, impassable wall of thorny vines rises suddenly to the south and east,
standing approximately fifteen feet tall. The thorns covering the green vines
are long and sharp. The area immediately surrounding the wall is filled with
dense, treacherous vegetation. A narrow hole can be seen in the wall to the
east, where the vines are thinner and can possibly be opened to allow passage.
name: Wall of Thorns

desc:
Tall hills rise up around this area. To the west, open plains travel between the
hills, creating a valley that goes on for miles. Pieces of an overgrown path can
be seen leading into the valley, but it looks to have been out of use for ages.
South of here can be seen a field of tall grasses.
name: Entrance to a Valley

desc:
A steep, grassy hill rises sharply out of the earth directly north of here. It
is strewn with rocks and shrubs that look like they would provide ample
handholds and footholds for someone wanting to venture upwards. Elsewise, the
valley floor can be followed east or west.
name: Beneath a Rocky Slope

desc:
Grassy hills of varying height roll up and down in every direction. Some cannot
be climbed because they are too steep, or their soil is too loose, or there is
not a path that is not too rocky. Those that can be climbed too often offer
nothing more than a view of more hills.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
This area is filled with several grassy hills. To the east and south, two hills
rise imposingly, their steep slopes covered by jagged red rocks that seem to
protrude in a threatening manner. It is as if the hills are great beasts with
spikes growing from their skin, but they remain as unmoving as the land around
them.
name: Hills of Jagged Stone

desc:
A valley bends through the surrounding hills, travelling north and east from
here. There was a gravel road or trail here once, but now only pieces of it can
be seen amongst the grass. Most of it washed away or disappeared into the earth
decades ago.
name: Bending Valley

desc:
Hills rise up east and west of here, terminating at plateus. Their banks are too
eroded to climb. Occasionally, the remains of an old, rocky path or trail can be
seen peeking through the grass, following the valley north and south.
name: Valley between Plateaus

desc:
A valley winds its way west and south between some hills. In this area are
several stone foundations, gravel paths, and a collapsed well. This was once a
small village, but it has been abandoned for decades. To the east, a partially
overgrown stone path climbs a hill.
name: Ruins of a Village

desc:
This valley winds its way east and west through grassy hills that rise up around
here. A gentle incline offers easy passage up a lightly wooded hill to the
south. The hills to the north are tall and densely wooded, giving wildlife ample
refuge from the openness of the valley.
name: Valley through Some Hills

desc:
A valley winds its way between hills that rise to the north and south of here.
When not frozen, the moss-covered ground is soft and spongy from moisture that
rolls down into the plains from the surrounding hills. Clusters of tall ferns
rise from the earth in a few places.
name: Moss Covered Valley

desc:
This hill rises above the surrounding prairie, with eroded slopes impeding
travel everywhere except to the north. At the very top of the hill are the
remains of a simple stone tower. It has collapsed and only its bottom third
still stands. In the distance, several miles to the west, can be seen a large
stone structure. To the east can be seen a plateau on the other side of a
valley, rocky ruins of some sort evident on its top.
name: Remains of a Tower on a Knoll

desc:
The open plains here are dotted with unusual shrubs, their twisting branches and
vibrant leaves catching the eye. They have a distinctive shape, their branches
spiralling upwards like the flames of a fire. The air here is fresh and crisp,
smelling of pine and earth.
name: Shrub Covered Plains

desc:
The open plains here are dotted with clusters of trees and shrubs, providing
pockets of shade in an otherwise sun-soaked landscape. To the north, towering
hills rise abruptly from the earth, their rocky peaks scraping the sky. The
plains are criss-crossed by a network of game trails, and occasionally the
distant calls of predators and prey can be heard echoing across the landscape.
name: Plains along Towering Hills

desc:
Open plains stretch out as far as the eye can see. They are filled with with
tall blades of golden grass waving in the gentle breeze, dotted with jagged
rocks and a patchwork of evergreen trees and shrubs. When wind whispers across
the prairie, it carries the scent of pine and earth with it.
name: Patchwork Prairie

desc:
An ancient stone temple, the size of a small keep, stands directly west of here.
A gaping hole with no doors offers easy entry. A rusted, iron fence connects to
the temple and travels east. High above to the left of the entrance can be see a
tower with glassless windows overlooking the fenced area.
name: In Front of an Ancient Temple

desc:
A ten foot tall iron gate supported by stone pillars stands south of here. A
rusting iron fence stretches out from it and travels far to the west and east.
West of here, a stone structure the size of a small keep rises up from the
surrounding prairie.
name: Before a Dark Iron Fence

desc:
During the spring, the open plains here are dotted with wildflowers, creating a
sea of colour as far as the eye can see. The hills to the east rise gently and
roll gracefully towards the horizon. The sounds of birds and other wildlife fill
the air.
name: Flowering Field

desc:
A rusting iron fence, at least eight feet tall and supported by stone pillars,
stands south of here. It travels west as far as can be seen. To the east it
turns south and out of view. Open plains stretch out around here, offering light
and easy travel.
name: Along a Dark Iron Fence

desc:
A valley filled with lush grass is nestled within rough, rocky hills. Boulders
and a few small trees dot the landscape. During the spring and summer, this area
is filled with blooming wildflowers. Near one of the hills is the burnt husk of
what was once a small hunting cabin. Nothing but charred wood remains.
name: Valley beneath Rocky Hills

desc:
A dozen or so pine trees are clustered here, in the middle of an otherwise empty
plain. The grass near the trees is matted down in several places where deer or
elk have slept. Rocky hills rise all around this area, making travel difficult
in any direction except the west.
name: Grove of Pine Trees

desc:
This room is the base of a tower, about eight feet square and thirty feet high.
A doorway leads to a larger room that is adjacent to the west. A sturdy
staircase built of stone brackets and oak treads is built into the walls,
spiralling upward.
name: Base of a Spiral Staircase

desc:
The top of this tower is a room about eight foot square. The walls and ceiling
are stone. A wooden platform offers a place to stand in most of the room, aside
from the southern wall where a staircase begins its descent. Large, open windows
in the east and west walls offer expansive views of the surrounding lands, as
well as the rising and setting of the sun. A heavy wooden headstock, from which
a bell can be hung, is attached near the ceiling.
name: Atop a Bell Tower

desc:
Brickwork arches support the walls and ceiling of this room. It looks like it
was built as a continuation of the crypt to the west, but there are no
sarcophagi here. Instead, there are several wooden shelves that once held
linnens and supplies, but their contents have been ransacked, and little
remains.
name: Undercroft

desc:
This is the southwest corner of some large burial grounds, marked by a rusting
iron fence that travels east and north from a stone pillar. Near the pillar are
the remains of a tomb, recessed into the earth. Its roof has collapsed, creating
a dark portal leading down.
name: Corner of Some Burial Grounds

desc:
Barrows of varying heights dot the landscape north of here. Taller barrows
hinder travel to the east. West of here stands the remains of an ancient iron
fence. Near the fence, several stone tombs are built, recessed into the earth.
Their entrances are blocked and sealed by piles of stone.
name: Tombs and Barrows

desc:
The walls of an ancient stone temple rise high above the ground, terminating at
the top of a tower overlooking these burial grounds. A rusted, iron fence
connects to the temple walls and travels east and south. Mounds of earth up to
ten feet tall dot the land south and east of here.
name: Corner of an Ancient Temple

desc:
A ten foot tall iron gate supported by stone pillars stands north of here,
marking the entrance of these burial grounds. In all other directions there are
mounds of earth of varying sizes, many adorned by flat, grey stones. West of
here, a stone structure the size of a small keep rises up behind the barrows.
name: Burial Grounds

desc:
This is the northeast corner of a large burial grounds, marked by a tall stone
pillar and a rusting iron fence that travels west and south. A circular section
of the ground here, about a hundred feet in diameter, is made of cobblestone.
The once ornate surface has been overrun by grasses growing between its stones.
name: Remains of a Gazebo

desc:
There is evidence that many people were lain to rest in this area centuries ago.
Barrows range in sizes from small enough to cover only a single man, to mounds
of earth large enough to cover multiple generations of a family. No evidence
exists of recent usage, as the barrows and the ground between are covered in
thick, untamed grass and spotted with thorny bushes. No clear path exists, but
travel between the barrows is possible in any direction.
name: Field of Barrows

desc:
Amongst the downs is an area of circular cobblestone that has been overrun by
grass. It is surrounded by twisted, unkempt bushes that are the remains of what
was once a manicured hedge. South of here is a small hill with a gaping entrance
facing a statue, a stone stairway leading down and within.
name: Weathered Monument

desc:
Flat, rocky terrain extends far into the distance to the north and east, dotted
with the occasional jagged outcropping of stone. To the south is a field of tall
briars that would be impossible to navigate. The ground is hard-packed and
challenging to traverse, with loose rocks and boulders scattered throughout. The
remains of a broken fence can be seen west of here.
name: Flat, Rocky Brush

desc:
The ground here is a mix of sand and soil, covered with rocks and brush. A few
trees dot the landscape in all directions. West of here, a tall fence of iron
rises. It is hard to tell what is contained within. The rocky ground stretches
out to the east and south.
name: Sandy Brushland

desc:
An old well whose interior has collapsed sits in the middle of four stone
foundations. This is the remains of a small settlement that has been abandoned
for decades. The remnants of a gravel trail can be seen leading eastward towards
a tall, grey rise of stone in the distance.
name: Remains of an Old Settlement

desc:
A wide channel has been carved into this eighty foot high hump of grey rock that
protrudes from the plains. On all sides are nearly sheer walls, save for a few
man-made shelves. The remains of simple but tall wooden ladders still lean
against the walls in places. Scattered about are bricks of various sizes. Brush
and weeds grow from cracks and crevices throughout.
name: Ancient Stone Quarry

desc:
A group of boulders, averaging around ten feet in height, stands tall and proud
here. The air is dry and crisp. Wind occassionally rushes past the boulders,
breaking the stillness with whispers. To the south and east, thick briars block
passage, forming an impenetrable barrier.
name: Stand of Stones

desc:
This area is filled with green barrows, grey name-stones, and black briars. The
thorny brush becomes taller and thicker as one travels east. To the south, a
tall fence of iron and stone pillars stands as if purpose built to keep the dead
within their graves. The burial grounds continue to the west and north.
name: Field of Barrows

desc:
Thorny briars grow thick and tall in this area, save for a single passage around
ten feet wide that winds its way through. The bushes thin out as one travels
west, but become thicker still to the east. In spite of the wildness of this
place, the air is eerily void of the sounds of animals.
name: Passage through Some Thorns

desc:
